name: CFFI (C Foreign Function Interface for Python)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); originally by Armin Rigo/Maciej Fijalkowski

performance.notes: Near-native C call overhead; ctypes compatibility; used in PyPy

agent_readiness.notes: Call C code from Python via inline declarations or ABI mode. from cffi import FFI. ffi = FFI(). ffi.cdef('int add(int, int);'). lib = ffi.dlopen('./mylib.so'). lib.add(1, 2). ABI mode (no compilation): parse headers, call shared library. API mode (with compilation): generate C extension with type safety. Used by cryptography, pyOpenSSL, and many system libraries. More robust than ctypes for complex APIs. PyPy-compatible. Handles pointers, structs, callbacks, memory management.

gotchas: ABI mode can crash on struct layout mismatches (prefer API mode)
Memory management (malloc/free) is manual — easy to leak
Error messages on type mismatches can be cryptic
Build setup for API mode adds complexity (cffi as build dependency)
best_when: Wrapping existing C shared libraries (.so/.dll) for Python use
When ctypes is insufficient for complex struct/pointer APIs
PyPy-compatible C bindings (ctypes is slower in PyPy)
avoid_when: Writing new C extensions (pybind11 or nanobind more ergonomic)
summary: CFFI provides C Foreign Function Interface for Python — call C shared libraries from Python. ffi = FFI(); ffi.cdef('int fn(int, int);'); lib = ffi.dlopen('./lib.so'); lib.fn(1, 2). Two modes: ABI (call .so directly, no compilation) and API (generate C extension, type-safe). More robust than ctypes for complex struct/pointer APIs. PyPy-compatible. Used by cryptography, pyOpenSSL. Handles pointers, structs, callbacks, manual memory management.
